title: Che Guevara
summary: The Argentine doctor turned Marxist guerrilla who helped Fidel Castro win the Cuban Revolution, served in its new government and oversaw the revolutionary tribunals and executions at La Cabaña, theorized armed revolution for the whole Third World, died trying to spread it in Bolivia in 1967, and was reborn as one of the most reproduced photographs of the twentieth century — a face that means rebellion to millions, ruthlessness to many others, and money to the companies that sell it.
hook: Che Guevara is the revolutionary who became a T-shirt. An Argentine doctor, he gave up a comfortable life to make revolution across three continents. He helped Fidel Castro seize Cuba, then oversaw the revolutionary tribunals at La Cabaña, where he confirmed the death sentences that sent prisoners to the firing squad. Restless, he left to spread the fight abroad, and in 1967 the Bolivian army, aided by CIA advisers, captured him, and a Bolivian soldier then executed him. Then his face went up on a billion posters and shirts, stripped of his politics, sold for profit. To millions he is a hero and a martyr who died for the world's poor. To many others he was a hardliner who believed in revolutionary violence and built nothing free. Both pictures point at the same man, and the argument between them has never settled.

beginner.theFigure:
In October 1967, in a small schoolhouse in the Bolivian hills, a soldier was ordered to shoot a captured prisoner. The prisoner was a wounded man with a thick beard and asthma. His name was Ernesto Guevara, but the whole world knew him as Che. He had once been a doctor. He had given that up to become a guerrilla fighter, and he had spent ten years trying to bring revolution to the poor of the world.

Che was not Cuban. He was from Argentina, born into a middle-class family. As a young man he traveled across South America and was shocked by the poverty he saw. He decided that only armed revolution could fix it. He joined Fidel Castro's rebels and helped them take over Cuba in 1959.

In the new government, Che held important jobs. He also ran the trials and executions of people from the old regime at a fort called La Cabaña. Then he left Cuba to start revolutions in other countries. He failed, and in Bolivia he was caught and killed. He was thirty-nine years old.
beginner.worldInherited:
Che grew up in a Latin America split sharply between rich and poor. A small group of families owned much of the land. Huge numbers of people had almost nothing. Many could not read. Many had no doctor and no school.

Much of the region also lived in the shadow of the United States. American companies owned mines, plantations, and oil. When a government tried to challenge them, it often fell. In 1954, Che was in Guatemala when a US-backed coup overthrew a reformist president there. That event marked him deeply. He decided that gentle reform would always be crushed.

The wider world was frozen in the Cold War. The United States and the Soviet Union, two giant powers, fought for control of the globe without fighting each other directly. Each backed friendly governments and rebels. Che came to believe that the poor countries of the world, what people then called the Third World, could only be free through revolution. That would mean armed struggle against the rich and against the United States.
beginner.howExercisedPower:
Che first held power as a fighter. In Cuba's mountains he proved brave and harsh. He enforced strict discipline and was known to deal severely with those he saw as traitors. He rose to become one of Castro's top commanders, and his column helped win the war.

When the rebels took power in 1959, Che was given the job of cleaning out the old regime. At La Cabaña fortress, he oversaw trials of former police, soldiers, and officials. Many were found guilty and shot. Supporters say these men had tortured and killed under the old dictator. Critics say the trials were swift and unfair, and that Che showed no mercy. He did not hide his belief that revolution required killing its enemies.

Later he ran Cuba's national bank and its industry. He pushed a bold idea. People should work hard not for money but out of duty to others. He called this the goal of building a "New Man." He even cut sugar cane on weekends to set an example. The economy struggled, and his plans contributed to shortages and inefficiency.
beginner.whatTheyChanged:
Che helped turn Cuba into a communist state and a thorn in the side of the United States. That was real and lasting. But his larger dream was to spread revolution everywhere, and there he mostly failed.

He grew restless ruling from a desk. He wanted to fight. In 1965 he secretly left Cuba and went to Africa, to the Congo, to help rebels there. The mission fell apart, and he called it a failure. Then he went to Bolivia to start a guerrilla war. The local people did not join him. He was hunted down, captured, and killed in 1967.

His theory of revolution traveled even when he did not. He taught that a small band of fighters could spark a wider uprising. Young rebels across Latin America tried it. Most were crushed, and many died. The pattern he promised did not hold outside Cuba.

What truly spread was his image. After his death he became a worldwide symbol of rebellion, printed on millions of posters and shirts.
beginner.legendVsRecord:
There are two Che Guevaras, and people argue about which is real.

The first is the hero. He is the doctor who gave up comfort to fight for the poor. He turned down power and an easy life to keep fighting. He died young, in a far country, for his beliefs. To millions, that makes him a martyr, a saint of rebellion. His face on a wall still means: rise up.

The second is the hardliner. He believed in violence as a tool of revolution and said so plainly. He approved the death sentences handed down at La Cabaña. He admired a strict, single-party state and had little patience for free debate. His wars abroad failed and got people killed. To his critics, the hero story hides a cold and ruthless man.

There is a strange twist at the end. Che hated money and profit. Yet his face became a product, sold for cash by companies around the world. The shirts rarely say what he actually did or believed.

general.theFigure:
Picture a young Argentine on a sputtering motorcycle, crossing South America in 1952 with a friend and almost no money. He is a medical student from a comfortable family. Along the road he sees mines that swallow workers, lepers cast out from society, and peasants worked into the ground. The trip turns him. Within a few years that traveler, Ernesto Guevara, has decided that charity and reform are useless against such poverty, and that only armed revolution can change it.

The man the world calls Che compressed several lives into thirty-nine years. He was a doctor who became a guerrilla, a commander in the Cuban Revolution, a minister in its government, a theorist of world revolution, and finally a fighter killed in the Bolivian hills. After his death he became something none of these roles predicted: the most reproduced face of the century, a global brand of rebellion. The gap between the man and the icon is the central puzzle of his story. The record shows real courage, real conviction, and a willingness to kill for the cause that his admirers tend to pass over.
general.worldInherited:
The Latin America that shaped Che was a region of stark inequality and heavy foreign influence. Land and wealth sat with a narrow elite, while millions lived without schooling, medicine, or secure work. Across the region, US companies controlled key industries, and Washington tended to treat the hemisphere as its backyard. Reformers who threatened American interests often found themselves out of power fast.

One event hardened Che above all others. In 1954 he was living in Guatemala, where a left-leaning president, Jacobo Árbenz, had begun land reform that touched the holdings of a powerful US fruit company. A CIA-backed coup removed Árbenz that year. Che watched a peaceful reform government fall to outside force, and he drew a lasting lesson: the United States would never allow gradual change, so change would have to come by the gun.

All of this unfolded inside the Cold War. The standoff between the United States and the Soviet Union turned every local struggle into a front in a larger contest. Che came to see the poor nations of the Third World as the decisive battleground, and armed revolution as the only path to free them from both their own elites and American power. That conviction, more than any single doctrine, organized the rest of his life.
general.howExercisedPower:
Che exercised power first as a soldier and then as an official, and he was unsentimental in both roles. In Cuba's Sierra Maestra he earned a reputation for physical courage and for harsh discipline, including the execution of men he judged to be informers or deserters. He became one of Castro's most trusted commanders, and his forces played a decisive part in the rebels' final push.

After the victory of January 1959, Castro put Che in charge of the purge of the old order at the La Cabaña fortress in Havana. There Che supervised revolutionary tribunals that tried former officials, police, and soldiers of the Batista dictatorship, and he confirmed death sentences that sent many to the firing squad. Defenders argue that the accused were torturers and killers, and that the trials answered popular demand for justice. Critics describe rushed proceedings with little defense and call the toll an early sign of the state's contempt for legal process. The exact number executed under his authority is disputed and is most often estimated in the dozens to low hundreds. Che never denied the killings; he defended revolutionary violence as a duty.

In government he ran the national bank and then the Ministry of Industry. He championed moral over material incentives, arguing that a true revolution would forge a "New Man" who labored out of solidarity rather than for wages. He led volunteer work brigades himself. The results were poor: his rapid push to industrialize and his distrust of markets contributed to shortages and inefficiency that dogged the Cuban economy.
general.whatTheyChanged:
Che's lasting concrete achievement was his part in building the Cuban state, the Americas' first communist government and a fixed irritant to Washington for decades. The deeper revolution of his life, the world revolution he preached, did not arrive.

Deskwork did not suit him, and by the mid-1960s he had left Cuban office to fight again. In 1965 he went secretly to the Congo to aid rebels there, an effort that collapsed within months and that he judged a failure. He then turned to Bolivia, where in 1966 he tried to ignite a guerrilla war. The local peasantry did not rally to him, the Bolivian army hunted his small band, and in October 1967 he was captured and executed, with the Bolivian forces aided by the CIA.

His theory outran his campaigns. In his writings he argued that a small armed nucleus, a foco, could create the conditions for revolution rather than waiting for them, and could draw the masses into a wider war. Inspired guerrillas across Latin America tried the method in the 1960s and 1970s. Almost everywhere it failed, often catastrophically, as states crushed isolated bands cut off from the populations they meant to lead.

What spread without limit was his image, which became the durable medium of his influence after the man was gone.
general.legendVsRecord:
Few modern figures have been remade as thoroughly as Che Guevara, and the remaking began at the moment of his death. The agent of it was a photograph. In 1960 the Cuban photographer Alberto Korda caught Che at a memorial service, gazing out beneath a beret with an expression of grave intensity. The image, later titled Guerrillero Heroico, went largely unused until 1967. After he was killed, it spread across the world and became, by most accounts, the most reproduced photograph of the twentieth century.

The legend it carries is the hero and martyr: the privileged doctor who renounced comfort and even power to fight for the wretched of the earth, and who died for it far from home. That reading has real foundation. Che genuinely gave up rank and safety, and he genuinely died for his cause. The competing reading is the ruthless authoritarian: a man who preached and practiced revolutionary violence, who oversaw the executions at La Cabaña, who admired a disciplined one-party state and dismissed liberal freedoms, and whose guerrilla doctrine led many to their deaths. The evidence supports parts of both, and scholars such as Jon Lee Anderson have argued that the contradictions belong to a single coherent man rather than to a misunderstanding.

The sharpest irony lies in the afterlife. Che despised commerce and the profit motive. Yet his face has become a commodity sold on shirts, mugs, and posters by the very kind of companies he fought, usually drained of any politics at all. The icon of anti-capitalism became one of capitalism's reliable products, and the gap between what the image sells and what the man did is rarely closed for the buyer.

advanced.theFigure: The interpretive crux of the Guevara literature is whether the icon and the executioner can be held in a single frame without either dissolving the other, and the most rigorous biographies insist that they must be. The romantic reduction treats La Cabaña as an aberration or a youthful excess incidental to the figure who matters; the prosecutorial reduction treats the self-sacrifice and the medical idealism as a mask over a fanatic. Both evade the harder claim, advanced most fully by Jon Lee Anderson's archival biography, that Guevara's willingness to kill for the revolution and his willingness to die for it issued from the same austere moral absolutism. The asthma-ridden ascetic who held himself and others to a punishing standard of revolutionary purity is continuous with the tribunal officer who signed death sentences without apparent doubt. The analytical error is to assume that recognizing the idealism requires discounting the violence, or the reverse, when the more demanding task is to grasp them as expressions of one ethic that subordinated the individual entirely to a historical project.
advanced.worldInherited: Guevara's formation cannot be reduced to Marxist conversion narratives without flattening what was distinctive in it. His radicalization drew on a continental rather than a national frame; the journeys recorded in his diaries produced an identification with a pan-Latin American poor that preceded any systematic Marxism and that always sat in tension with the nation-state vehicles his revolutions actually used. The Guatemalan coup of 1954, in which the Árbenz government's reformism was undone by external intervention, supplied the empirical premise from which he reasoned: that the path of constitutional reform was foreclosed by the structure of US hemispheric power, so that armed struggle was not one option among several but the only one the system permitted. Whether this inference was warranted is precisely the point at which his admirers and critics divide. The reading that vindicates it treats the subsequent history of crushed reform governments as confirmation; the reading that rejects it notes that the armed path he prescribed produced even higher costs and fewer durable gains, and that he generalized from a particular conjuncture into a universal law of liberation.
advanced.howExercisedPower: The La Cabaña tribunals are the densest evidentiary problem in assessing Guevara, because the record is both real and politically saturated. That executions occurred under his authority in early 1959 is not in dispute; the contested questions are the numbers, the fairness of the proceedings, and the moral weight to assign them. Exile sources have offered figures running into the many hundreds; defenders cite far lower counts and emphasize the prior crimes of the accused under Batista. The proceedings combined genuine popular demand for retribution against torturers with summary process that afforded little defense, and the attempt to resolve the matter into either a legitimate reckoning or a massacre imposes a clarity the sources do not support. What the evidence does establish is Guevara's own framing: he did not regard the killings as regrettable necessities to be minimized but as obligations of a revolution that recognized class enemies as enemies. His later defense of revolutionary violence, including the much-quoted call to hatred as a factor in struggle, is consistent with this, and it is the element of his thought that the iconographic tradition most thoroughly suppresses.
advanced.whatTheyChanged: Foco theory, as codified in Guevara's Guerrilla Warfare and elaborated by Régis Debray, made a strong and largely falsified claim about revolutionary causation: that a committed armed nucleus could itself generate the subjective conditions for insurrection rather than waiting on objective ripeness. The Cuban victory appeared to license the inference, but the inference mistook a singular conjuncture for a replicable model. Subsequent foco-inspired insurgencies across Latin America failed with grim regularity, and Guevara's own Bolivian campaign became the most direct refutation of his theory, undone by the very factors the theory discounted: the absence of a sympathetic peasantry, the isolation of the guerrilla from the population, hostile terrain, and an adversary trained and equipped by the United States. The deeper analytical question is whether Cuba itself was won by foco logic or by a broader urban and rural coalition that the theory's emphasis on the rural guerrilla nucleus retrospectively obscured. If the Cuban case was misread by its own protagonist, then the doctrine he exported rested on a mistaken account of the revolution that produced him.
advanced.legendVsRecord: The Korda image is the rare instance where the mechanism of mythmaking can be reconstructed with precision, and its trajectory exposes the political economy that the romantic reading occludes. Korda received no payment for an image that generated vast commercial value, a fact often cited as poetic justice against the figure of a commercial society; the deeper irony is that the anti-capitalist icon became a commodity form, circulating as pure signifier detached from the executions, the failed campaigns, and the authoritarian commitments of the man depicted. The contemporary scholarship on the image, including work by Michael Casey, treats this evacuation of content as constitutive rather than incidental: the icon functions precisely because it has been emptied, available to be filled by whatever rebellion the wearer projects onto it. This produces a genuine interpretive stake. To read Guevara through the image is to encounter a figure already stripped of the features that made him historically consequential and morally contested, so that the recovery of the actual Guevara, idealist and executioner at once, requires reading against the most successful act of branding the left ever produced, and one it never intended.

foundation.theStory: Che Guevara matters as the figure who makes visible the full moral complexity of twentieth-century revolution: the self-sacrificing idealism and the readiness to kill that coexisted in a single life. He matters as a central commander of the Cuban Revolution, as the theorist whose guerrilla doctrine shaped and often doomed insurgencies across Latin America, and as a singular case of a radical turned, against everything he believed, into a mass-market commodity. Understanding him requires recovering the historical actor from beneath both the heroic icon and the hostile caricature.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern how to weigh Guevara's idealism against the executions he oversaw at La Cabaña; whether his theory of armed struggle was a realistic reading of Latin American conditions or a tragic miscalculation generalized from the unrepeatable Cuban case; and how the Korda image transformed a contested revolutionary into a depoliticized global brand. These debates remain charged because they bear on whether Che is best remembered as a martyr for the poor or as a hardliner whose methods failed bloodily.
foundation.whyItMatters: Che Guevara matters for understanding the Cuban Revolution and the Cold War in Latin America, the relationship between revolutionary idealism and revolutionary violence, the theory and failure of guerrilla insurgency in the 1960s, and the way images detach from the people they depict and acquire lives of their own in modern culture.
questions: Che Guevara gave up a comfortable life to fight for the poor, and he also confirmed death sentences at La Cabaña. Can both of these be true of the same person, and how should we hold them together when we judge him? What kinds of evidence would help you weigh his idealism against the violence he carried out?

Guevara believed a small band of fighters could spark a wider revolution, a method that worked in Cuba and failed almost everywhere else. Why might a strategy succeed once and then fail repeatedly, and what does that suggest about the danger of generalizing from a single dramatic success?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Scholarship on Guevara divides along several axes. Jon Lee Anderson's archival biography is the standard comprehensive treatment, attentive to both the idealism and the violence and insistent on their unity. Jorge Castañeda's account emphasizes the political failures and the gap between Guevara's self-image and his results, while Paco Ignacio Taibo II offers a more sympathetic narrative. A separate literature, including Michael Casey's work, examines the afterlife of the Korda image as a study in branding and depoliticization. Sympathetic and critical accounts diverge most sharply over La Cabaña and over whether foco theory was a serious strategic doctrine or a romantic error.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidentiary situation combines rich personal sources with politically saturated contested specifics. Guevara left extensive diaries, letters, and theoretical writings, and his campaigns are well documented. But the central contested facts, especially the number executed under his authority at La Cabaña and the fairness of those tribunals, vary systematically with the politics of the source, since the Cuban state and the exile community curate the record around opposed narratives. The internal reasoning behind his decisions is partly accessible through his own writings, which are themselves shaped for political effect. The result is a field in which the outline of his life is secure while several morally decisive specifics remain genuinely open.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible position on Guevara must resist two symmetrical temptations: treating the idealism and self-sacrifice as a verdict that dissolves the executions, and treating the executions as a verdict that erases everything else. The stronger move, following Anderson, is to hold both as expressions of a single revolutionary ethic that subordinated the individual to the cause, and to be explicit about the criteria one is using to judge it. On foco theory, a strong argument cannot rest on the Cuban success alone, since that case may have been won by a broader coalition than the theory credits; it must engage the pattern of failure elsewhere, above all in Bolivia, where Guevara's own death refuted his doctrine on the points it most discounted. The position one can most defend specifies its standards openly and concedes where the record runs out.
questions: The same Korda photograph that celebrates Che as a rebel is sold for profit by companies on T-shirts and posters, usually with no mention of his actual politics. What does it tell us about modern culture that an anti-capitalist revolutionary became one of capitalism's best-selling images, and what is lost when an image floats free of the person it shows?

Guevara defended revolutionary violence openly and did not present the La Cabaña executions as regrettable necessities to be minimized. How should historians assess a figure who was candid about the violence he carried out, as opposed to one who concealed it, and does that candor change the moral reckoning?

research.topic: Guevara's role in the Cuban Revolution and the early revolutionary government
research.content: The basic arc of Guevara's career is well documented: his radicalization during travels across Latin America and during the 1954 Guatemalan coup, his joining Castro's expedition aboard the Granma in 1956, his rise to comandante in the Sierra Maestra guerrilla war, and his senior positions in the new state after January 1959, including oversight of the La Cabaña tribunals, the presidency of the national bank, and the Ministry of Industry. His authorship of the foco doctrine in Guerrilla Warfare, his secret 1965 Congo mission, and his 1966–67 Bolivian campaign ending in his capture and execution are likewise established through his own diaries, contemporary records, and multiple biographies.

research.topic: The Korda image and the political economy of its afterlife
research.content: Scholarship on Guerrillero Heroico, including Michael Casey's study of the photograph, has examined how a 1960 image by Alberto Korda became, after Guevara's 1967 death, one of the most reproduced photographs in the world and a depoliticized global commodity. This work treats the evacuation of political content as central to the image's commercial success rather than incidental to it, and traces the circulation of the icon across protest movements and consumer markets alike. The analysis of how revolutionary images detach from their referents and acquire independent commercial lives remains an active area of cultural scholarship.

research.topic: The number executed under Guevara's authority at La Cabaña and the conduct of the tribunals
research.content: The precise number of people executed under Guevara's authority at La Cabaña in 1959, and the fairness of the revolutionary tribunals he supervised, cannot be confidently resolved. What evidence would resolve it: complete, uncurated trial records and execution registers from the period, independently verified. Why unavailable: the Cuban state controls the central archives and has curated the record around the official narrative, while exile sources advance higher figures shaped by an opposed politics, and contemporary documentation was incomplete. What can be responsibly inferred: that executions occurred under his authority, that many of the accused were officials of the Batista regime charged with torture and killing, that the proceedings afforded limited defense, and that Guevara defended the killings rather than minimizing them. What must remain genuinely uncertain: the exact toll and the degree of due process in individual cases.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Martyr for the Poor
comparativeNarrative.content: To much of the Left and to anti-imperial movements, Che is a hero and a martyr. In this telling, he gave up comfort and even high office to fight for the world's poor. He refused to settle into power, and he died young in a foreign country for his beliefs. His call for a selfless New Man, his solidarity with the Third World, and his death at the hands of a CIA-aided army make him a saint of rebellion. The framing does real work. It casts his violence as the hard cost of a just war against cruelty, and his failures as noble sacrifices rather than mistakes. It shapes how generations of activists have understood what commitment means.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Ruthless Authoritarian
comparativeNarrative.content: To his critics, the hero story hides a hardliner. In this account, Guevara believed in revolutionary violence and said so without apology. He ran the swift trials and executions at La Cabaña. He admired a strict one-party state and had little use for free speech or open debate. His economic ideas helped wreck Cuban production. His theory of armed struggle led young idealists across Latin America to their deaths. This view, strong among Cuban exiles, insists that a man be judged by what he did and built, not by the cause he professed. It carries the weight of those his revolution jailed or exiled, though it can pass too lightly over the brutal regimes the rebels rose against.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Face Without the Man
comparativeNarrative.content: A third vantage steps back from the political quarrel to watch what the world did with Che after he died. The striking fact, from here, is that an anti-capitalist became one of capitalism's best-selling images. The Korda photograph, printed on a billion shirts, mugs, and posters, sells rebellion as a mood. It rarely mentions La Cabaña or Bolivia. Scholars of this afterlife argue that the image works because it has been emptied, ready to mean whatever the buyer wants. This view does not settle whether Che was hero or hardliner. It reveals what the political fight misses: how easily an image floats free of the person, and how a market can absorb even its own enemy and sell him back as a product.

comparativeSummary: What divides these accounts is not the basic facts of Guevara's life but the meaning assigned to them. The revolutionary tradition sees a martyr whose violence was the price of a just war; the critical tradition sees a ruthless authoritarian who must be judged by what he did and built; the cultural vantage steps outside the quarrel to watch an anti-capitalist become a commodity sold for profit. Each foregrounds one truth and discounts another, and each carries a stake that shapes what it remembers. Held together, they show why Che resists a verdict: the same man cut sugar cane in solidarity and confirmed death sentences at La Cabaña, and no single perspective holds both without strain.

rabbitHole.label: The Cuban Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Guevara was a central commander of the revolution that toppled Batista and built Cuba's new state — his career and his contested legacy are inseparable from the uprising whose course and consequences this entry traces in full.

rabbitHole.label: Communism
rabbitHole.reason: The Marxism Guevara embraced after 1954 drove his entire life and his vision of a selfless New Man motivated by moral rather than material incentives — understanding the doctrine is foundational to understanding what he fought to build.

rabbitHole.label: The Cold War
rabbitHole.reason: Guevara's bid to spread armed revolution across the Third World, and his death at the hands of a CIA-aided Bolivian army, were episodes in the global superpower contest that gave every local struggle of the era its larger stakes.

rabbitHole.label: Simón Bolívar
rabbitHole.reason: An earlier figure of armed Latin American liberation whom Guevara consciously echoed in his dream of a continent freed by force — the comparison sharpens what such liberation movements can win and what they characteristically fail to transform.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: The imperialism and economic dependency Guevara fought to overthrow across the Third World — his guerrilla doctrine was framed explicitly as a tool for liberating poor nations from the structures colonialism left behind.

rabbitHole.label: Liberation Theology
rabbitHole.reason: The radical Latin American current that shared Guevara's option for the poor and his openness to the path of struggle, drawing on the same period's confrontation with entrenched power and US-backed order in the region.

rabbitHole.label: Fidel Castro
rabbitHole.reason: The leader Guevara joined and helped bring to power, whose state Guevara served before leaving to fight abroad — Castro's long rule and pragmatism stand in pointed contrast to Guevara's restless drive to export revolution and die for it.

reference.author: Anderson, Jon Lee
reference.title: Che Guevara: A Revolutionary Life
reference.annotation: The standard comprehensive biography, built on extensive archival research and interviews, and the definitive statement of the reading that holds Guevara's idealism and his readiness to kill as expressions of a single ethic rather than a contradiction to be resolved away.

reference.author: Castañeda, Jorge G.
reference.title: Compañero: The Life and Death of Che Guevara
reference.annotation: Essential for the critical assessment of Guevara's political failures and the gap between his self-image and his results — Castañeda is especially clear-eyed about the strategic errors of foco theory and the Bolivian campaign.

reference.author: Taibo II, Paco Ignacio
reference.title: Guevara, Also Known as Che
reference.annotation: A detailed and more sympathetic narrative biography that supplies texture on the campaigns and the inner life, useful as a counterweight to the more critical accounts and for its command of the documentary record.

reference.author: Guevara, Ernesto Che
reference.title: Guerrilla Warfare (La guerra de guerrillas)
reference.annotation: Guevara's own codification of foco doctrine, the primary statement of the strategy the entry assesses as won once in Cuba and falsified almost everywhere else, including in his own death in Bolivia.

reference.author: Casey, Michael
reference.title: Che's Afterlife: The Legacy of an Image
reference.annotation: The essential study of the Korda photograph and its transformation into a global commodity, underpinning the entry's claim that the icon functions by being emptied of the political content that made the man historically consequential and contested.

reference.author: Gott, Richard
reference.title: Cuba: A New History
reference.annotation: Provides the broader Cuban context within which Guevara operated, sympathetic to the revolution's social aims and useful for situating his government posts and the new state's early consolidation.

commerce.title: The Motorcycle Diaries
commerce.author: Ernesto Che Guevara
commerce.note: Meet the young man before the legend — Guevara's own travel journal of the 1952 journey that radicalized him, vivid and accessible, though it ends long before the guerrilla and the tribunal officer he became. The natural first book.

commerce.title: Compañero: The Life and Death of Che Guevara
commerce.author: Jorge G. Castañeda
commerce.note: For the reader who wants a clear-eyed account of the politics — Castañeda is readable and unsparing about the failures, the best single book for understanding why Che's strategy mostly did not work.

commerce.title: Che Guevara: A Revolutionary Life
commerce.author: Jon Lee Anderson
commerce.note: Go here for the full, authoritative life — long and demanding, but the deepest archival treatment, essential for anyone who wants past both the poster and the caricature to the actual man.

commerce.title: Che's Afterlife: The Legacy of an Image
commerce.author: Michael Casey
commerce.note: Read this to understand how the face conquered the world — Casey traces the Korda photograph from a Havana memorial to a billion T-shirts, the best account of how an anti-capitalist became a commodity.

commerce.title: Cuba: A New History
commerce.author: Richard Gott
commerce.note: For the wider stage on which Che acted — a readable history of the revolution and the state he helped build, best paired with a more critical account to weigh the social gains against the costs.

popularCulture.title: The Motorcycle Diaries — Walter Salles (2004)
popularCulture.description: This film follows a young Che on a road trip across South America, watching poverty and injustice slowly turn him into a revolutionary. It is warm, beautiful, and moving. It stops before he ever picks up a gun. A viewer meets the gentle, searching young man and never sees the hard commander who later sent men to the firing squad at La Cabaña.

popularCulture.title: The Korda photograph on a billion shirts
popularCulture.description: A 1960 photo of Che, staring out under a beret, became one of the most printed images in the world, sold on T-shirts and posters everywhere. It turned a real fighter into a symbol of rebellion that almost anyone could wear. The image floats free of the man who hated profit. Companies sell his face for cash, and it rarely tells you what he actually did or believed.

popularCulture.title: Che — Steven Soderbergh (2008)
popularCulture.description: Two long films cover the Cuban war and the Bolivian campaign that killed Che. They show his discipline, his asthma, and the slow collapse of his last mission in close detail. The treatment is serious and admiring. The executions he oversaw in Havana after the victory get little attention, so the ruthless side of his record stays mostly out of frame.

popularCulture.title: Che as a saint of rebellion
popularCulture.description: To many on the Left and in poor countries, Che became a kind of secular saint, a young martyr who died for the world's poor. Murals, songs, and slogans treat him as pure conviction. The picture is powerful and partly true. It also leaves out his belief in revolutionary violence and the firing squads he confirmed, so the saint hides the harder man underneath.

popularCulture.title: Foco theory and the guerrillas who followed it
popularCulture.description: Che taught that a small band of fighters could spark a wider revolution. Young rebels across Latin America believed him and took up arms in the 1960s and 1970s. The idea had worked once, in Cuba. Almost everywhere else it failed, and many who tried it were hunted down and killed, including Che himself in Bolivia. The dream he sold proved deadly to those who bought it.
